pending_diagnosis: DIAGNOSIS: Acute Intermittent Porphyria (AIP) THE SMOKING GUN: The critical, often missed laboratory observation was the delayed analysis of a random urine sample. This sample, initially collected as part of the workup, appeared clear but spontaneously turned a dark reddish-purple, "port-wine" color upon standing and exposure to light due to the spontaneous conversion of highly elevated Porphobilinogen (PBG) into Porphobilin. Subsequent urgent specialized testing confirmed drastically elevated urinary PBG and Delta-Aminolevulinic Acid (ALA), confirming the diagnosis of an acute attack of AIP. The initial trigger was the use of Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole, a sulfa drug known to be a potent inducer of hepatic cytochrome P450 enzymes and therefore, a classic precipitant of Porphyria attacks. The Hyponatremia was likely due to SIADH, a known complication of AIP’s central nervous system involvement. PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: AIP is an autosomal dominant disorder resulting from a partial deficiency in the enzyme Hydroxymethylbilane Synthase (HMBS), also known as Porphobilinogen Deaminase (PBGD), the third enzyme in the heme biosynthesis pathway. When the pathway is upregulated (e.g., by fasting, alcohol, or CYP450-inducing drugs), the deficient enzyme cannot efficiently process intermediates. This results in the massive accumulation of the upstream precursors, ALA and PBG, which are neurotoxic. The symptoms are purely neurovisceral: severe, non-surgical abdominal pain (due to autonomic neuropathy affecting the gut), hypertension and tachycardia (autonomic instability), rapidly ascending motor neuropathy (due to toxicity to the peripheral nerves), and acute psychosis/seizures (CNS involvement). Immediate, life-saving treatment requires IV administration of Hemin (Panhematin) to suppress the primary enzyme ALA synthase, thereby halting the production of further toxic precursors.
